claims.claim: The ABSE does not consider the influence of load current on equivalent circuit model parameters, making it unsuitable for SOAP estimation in theory.
claims.quote: The ABSE does not consider the influence of load current on the equivalent circuit model parameters, so it is not suitable for SOAP estimation in theory.

claims.claim: The ABSE algorithm error source is that its identified Rp is much higher than the actual value when the battery is charged or discharged at a high current.
claims.quote: the ABSE algorithm error source lies in its identified Rp being much higher than the actual value when the battery is charged/discharged at a high current.

claims.claim: With aging of the Li-ion battery, the SOC and SOAPSOAB estimation errors of the ABSE increase.
claims.quote: with the aging of the Li-battery, the SOC and SOAPSOAB estimation errors of the ABSE increase.

claims.claim: The battery SOAPSOAB decreases significantly after Uout reaches the border of the safe operating area (Uout,max) during the TSOAP-second measurement period.
claims.quote: the battery SOAPSOAB decreases significantly after Uout reaches the border of the safe operating area (Uout,max), which is the reason for the strong oscillation of the SOAPch profiles when the SOC is high.

claims.claim: The improved ABSE-based open-loop battery model achieves a voltage estimation error of less than 35 mV in the entire UDDS discharging process and less than 5 mV in the entire 0.5C CCCV charging process.
claims.quote: the absolute value of the voltage estimation error is less than 35 mV in the entire UDDS discharging process and less than 5 mV in the entire 0.5C CCCV charging process.

claims.claim: Overcharge fault causes the serial resistance to increase to four times larger than the initial value, which is the most severe influence on both serial and parallel resistors among the three fault modes tested.
claims.quote: Overcharge obviously has the most severe inﬂuence on both serial and parallel resistors. The serial resistance is four times larger than the initial value.

claims.claim: Over-discharge and low-temperature faults both result in serial resistance approximately 1.5 times larger than the initial value, compared to four times for overcharge.
claims.quote: Over-discharge and low-temperature cells had a smaller serial resistance, about 1.5 times larger than the initial one.

claims.claim: In over-discharged cells, the serial resistance is uniform throughout the entire depth of discharge range, whereas in low-temperature cells the serial resistance is dependent on depth of discharge.
claims.quote: The serial resistance of an over-discharged cell is uniform throughout the entire depth of discharge (DOD) range and that of the low-temperature one is dependent on DOD.

claims.claim: Normal overcharge process may induce multiple degradation mechanisms: part of the degradation is caused by high temperature (ICA pattern 1) and the other part results from pure overcharge (ICA pattern 4), with high temperature contributing the majority of degradation in the general case.
claims.quote: the normal overcharge process may induce multiple degradation mechanisms: part of the degradation is caused by high temperature (pattern1) and the other part results from pure overcharge (pattern 4). High temperature contributes the majority of degradation in general case.

claims.claim: When the overcharge current rate was reduced to 0.1 C to limit temperature rise, a new ICA pattern (pattern 4) was observed in which peak I increases first and peak II decreases, which was not observed at the standard 1 C rate where temperature rose up to 65 °C.
claims.quote: In small rate test, peak ① II shown in Fig. 10 increases at ﬁrst and peak ② II decreases. This induces a new curve pattern and it is deﬁned as pattern 4. Compared with the results from Fig. 8, we put forward our standpoint that the normal overcharge process may induce multiple degradation mechanisms

claims.claim: Mathematical battery models provide inaccurate results in the order of 5%-20% error and cannot represent dynamic information correctly.
claims.quote: these models cannot represent the dynamic information correctly and provide inaccurate results in the order of 5%-20% error.

claims.claim: Under the HPPC test, the Three RC Network Model achieved the lowest average voltage relative error (0.1068%) and the 3rd Order RC Model with One-State Hysteresis achieved the second lowest (0.2300%), while the Nonlinear RC Model had the highest average voltage relative error (1.2211%).
The PNGV model produces long-term accumulation of voltage on the capacitor, which increases the model voltage error over long simulation times.
claims.quote: the PNGV model produces long-term accumulation of voltage on the capacitor, which increases the model voltage error.

claims.claim: The Nonlinear RC model cannot reflect the polarization phases of the Li-ion battery, which limits its accuracy in representing dynamic characteristics.
claims.quote: The Nonlinear RC model does not accurately reflect the dynamic characteristics of the battery because this model cannot reflect the polarization phases of the Li-ion battery. Therefore, its accuracy is limited.

claims.claim: Under the ECE15 test, the Three RC Network Model achieved the lowest average voltage relative error (0.1831%) and maximum error (1.2663%), while the Shepherd Model had the highest maximum voltage relative error (2.8041%).
All tested Ni-rich cells started their accelerated thermal runaway stage (T3) at the same self-heating temperature rate of approximately 48 °C/min, regardless of SOC level or cell technology.
claims.quote: all tested cells clearly exhibit stage 3 and stage 4, with the same temperature rate of ~6 °C/min at the beginning of vent port opening and ~48 °C/min at T3, regardless of SOC and cell technology

claims.claim: Lower SOC level delays the internal short circuit and shifts the initial self-heating as well as the activation of the accelerated thermal runaway stage to higher temperatures, causing T1, T2, and T3 to increase.
claims.quote: lower SOC level leads to ISC delay, and thereby shifts the initial self-heating as well as the activation of stage 4 to higher temperatures, as shown in Figure 13. Therefore, T1, T2, and T3 are increased for cells at reduced SOC.

claims.claim: Cells at 50% SOC showed lower maximum temperature after thermal runaway compared to 100% SOC cells; for LG HG2, maximum temperature was ~615 °C at 100% SOC and ~535 °C at 50% SOC.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature is also reduced with reduced SOC (~615 °C for LG HG2 100% SOC and ~535 °C for LG HG2 50% SOC)

claims.claim: The Panasonic NCR GA separator is approximately 38 µm thick with a ceramic-to-polymeric layer thickness ratio of ~1/2, which is significantly thicker than the LG HG2 separator at ~13 µm with a ceramic-to-polymeric ratio of ~1/12, and this thicker separator caused the hard ISC and final venting to occur at higher temperature (~205 °C vs. ~190 °C).
claims.quote: The separator total thickness is ~38 µm, which is significantly thicker than the separator for LG HG2. Therefore, the Panasonic NCR GA's separator fully collapses at higher temperature, which leads to the delay of final venting...and also to the delay of hard ISC (~205 °C compared to ~190 °C for LG HG2)

claims.claim: During final venting, the gasket seal collapsed only in cells at 50% SOC (not in 100% SOC cells), resulting in violent jelly roll ejection in 50% SOC cells, due to the shift of final venting to higher temperature with stronger exothermic reactions and higher pressure rate acceleration.
claims.quote: during final venting of all cells, the vent ports fully opened; however, the gasket seal collapsed only in the case of cells with 50% SOC...the pressure rate acceleration during stage 4 collapses the gasket seal. Therefore, the jelly roll of 50% SOC cells was violently ejected

claims.claim: NCA technology (Panasonic NCR GA) exhibited a higher self-heating temperature rate over a longer duration during stage 4 than NMC811 technology (LG HG2), regardless of SOC level, making NCA appear more reactive during the final stage of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the temperature rate during stage 4 of NCA technology reaches a higher value with longer duration, regardless of whether for 100% SOC or 50% SOC. In general, NCA technology appears to be more reactive during the final stage of the thermal runaway than that of NMC811 technology.

claims.claim: The peak surface temperatures caused by acupuncture, side heating, and overcharging are 774 °C, 835 °C, and 935 °C, respectively, for a 53 Ah NCM 721/graphite pouch cell at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: The peak surface temperatures caused by acupuncture, side heating, and overcharging are 774 °C, 835 °C, and 935 °C, respectively.

claims.claim: The injection (jet) durations for nail penetration, lateral heating, and overcharge thermal runaway are 22 s, 24 s, and 29 s, respectively, and injection time is positively correlated with energy input before thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the side needle trigger battery thermal runaway injection duration is 22 s, the lateral plus test injection duration is 24 s, and the overcharge injection time is 29 s... The injection time and the maximum surface temperature are proportional to the energy input before thermal runaway.

claims.claim: In the overcharge experiment at 1C from 100% SOC, thermal runaway was triggered when the surface temperature reached 117 °C, the battery was at 128.3% SOC at the time of thermal runaway, and the process took 1020 s.
claims.quote: It takes 1020 s to charge the battery from 100% SOC to thermal runaway at 1 C current, and the battery power is 128.3% SOC when it is out of control... When the surface temperature reached 117 °C, the thermal runaway was triggered.

claims.claim: During nail penetration thermal runaway, the internal temperature of the cell rises to 1005 °C while the maximum surface temperature is 774 °C, with the internal temperature rise occurring much earlier than the surface temperature rise.
claims.quote: The highest temperatures inside and outside of the cell are 1005 °C and 774 °C, respectively. The internal temperature rise is much earlier than the surface temperature rise, which indicates that the internal heat of the cell is out of control after nail penetration, and then diffuses from the inside to the outside.

claims.claim: Under lateral heating, the thermal runaway trigger surface temperature is 233 °C, the highest surface temperature is 835 °C, the highest internal temperature is 965 °C, and it takes about 20 s for thermal runaway to spread to the rear surface of the cell.
claims.quote: the trigger temperature is 233 °C, and the surface temperature of the cell after thermal runaway is mostly distributed in 750~790 °C... the highest surface temperature is 835 °C, and the highest internal temperature is 965 °C. It takes about 20 s for thermal runaway to spread to the rear surface of the cell.

claims.claim: As discharging rate increased from 0 C to 3 C, the maximum temperature rate of the battery module decreased from 154.1 °C/s to 38.4 °C/s, a reduction of nearly four times.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature rate drops from 154.1 °C/s to 38.4 °C/s which reflects a pronounced mitigation of TR intensity

claims.claim: As discharging rate increased to 3 C, TR propagation time extended from 779 s to 1163 s.
claims.quote: the propagation time extends from 779 s to 1163 s as the discharging rate increases to 3 C condition

claims.claim: Thermal conduction accounts for approximately 57–73% of the heat exchange between adjacent batteries during TR propagation in a parallel connection lithium-ion battery module.
claims.quote: thermal conduction plays a prominent role between adjacent batteries which accounts for approximately 57–73%

claims.claim: Under 3 C discharging condition, 2#, 3#, and 4# batteries no longer formed visible flame and instead released large amounts of white smoke throughout the TR propagation process.
claims.quote: When the discharging rate increased to 3 C, 2#, 3#, and 4# batteries no longer formed visible flame, instead of releasing larging amount of white smoke throughout the TR propagation process

claims.claim: TR propagation rate declined from 1.45 battery/min under without discharging condition to 0.5 battery/min at 3 C discharging rate, a reduction of nearly three times.
claims.quote: the parallel connection battery module propagation rate declined from 1.45 battery/min under without discharging condition to 0.5 battery/min at 3 C which reduced nearly three time

claims.claim: The thermal runaway onset temperature (Ttr) follows an upward trend as the environmental pressure decreases from 101 kPa to 30 kPa for 21700 NMC LIBs at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: Ttr follows an upward trend as the environmental pressure decreased from 101 to 30 kPa.

claims.claim: The time interval from safety venting to thermal runaway (tinterval) decreases from 48 s to 22 s when environmental pressure drops from 101 kPa to 30 kPa for 21700 NMC LIBs at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: tinterval reduced from 48 s to 22 s when the pressure declined from 101 to 30 kPa.

claims.claim: The maximum jet flame height of 21700 LIB thermal runaway jet fires reached its maximum value of 0.28 m at 50 kPa, and the phenomenon of flame lift-off was not observed at any of the tested pressures.
claims.quote: the phenomenon of flame lift-off was not observed at different pressures, and the jet flame height reached the maximum at 50 kPa, which was 0.28 m.

claims.claim: Environmental pressure has little significance on mass loss for 21700 LIBs, with mass loss values of 36.27 g, 39.93 g, 38.36 g, 41.70 g, and 33.16 g at 101, 90, 70, 50, and 30 kPa respectively, all at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: mass loss of 101 kPa, 90 kPa, 70 kPa, 50 kPa and 30 kPa were 36.27 g, 39.93 g, 38.36 g, 41.70 g and 33.16 g respectively. It indicated that environmental pressure has a little significance on mass loss for 21700 LIBs.

claims.claim: The temperature change rate (dT/dt) during thermal runaway of LIBs is related to both the internal pressure of the battery and the external environmental pressure, as derived from the heat transfer model.
claims.quote: the temperature change rate (dT/dt) is related to the internal and external pressure of the battery. Therefore, the rate of temperature rise during TR of LIBs is not only determined by the complex internal exothermic reaction but also related to the external environmental pressure.

claims.claim: T1 is the onset temperature for detectable self-heat generation in Li-ion batteries, and its mechanism is usually attributed to SEI decomposition.
claims.quote: T1 is the onset temperature for the detectable self-heat generation; the mechanism for this heat source is usually attributed to SEI decomposition.

claims.claim: A higher T2 (trigger temperature for thermal runaway) usually means better overall thermal stability, making the battery more likely to pass standard abuse tests.
claims.quote: T2 is the trigger temperature for TR. Understanding T2 is quite critical for safe battery design, because a higher T2 usually means better overall thermal stability, and the battery can thus be more likely to pass standard abuse test.

claims.claim: The total heat released during thermal runaway (∆HTR) is directly linked to the temperature difference ∆T=T3-T2, calculated as ∆HTR=M·Cp·(T3-T2), where M is cell mass and Cp is heat capacity.
claims.quote: The difference ∆T=T3-T2 is directly linked to the total heat released during a TR (∆HTR) as shown in Eqn. (1), where M is the cell mass and Cp is the heat capacity.

claims.claim: T3 (maximum temperature during thermal runaway) tends to be higher for cells with higher energy density.
claims.quote: T3 tends to be higher for cells with higher energy density (Fig. 3a).

claims.claim: The maximum temperature rise rate (max{dT·dt-1}) reported around T2 has a high correlation with the energy density of the battery, based on ARC test data from 33 battery samples with different cell chemistries.
claims.quote: The maximum dT·dt-1 that is reported around T2 has a high correlation with the energy density of the battery (Fig. 3b).

claims.claim: In the three-stage thermal runaway process: the anode+electrolyte system generates approximately 17% of total heat in Stage I, ISC contributes only 9% of total heat in Stage II, and the cathode-anode redox reaction releases 74% of total heat in Stage III.
claims.quote: The 'anode+electrolyte' system generates approximately 17% of the total heat released during TR in Stage I... Only 9% of the total heat released during TR is caused by ISC. Stage III: The redox reaction between the cathode and anode starts and release large amount of heat, 74% in this case.

claims.claim: T2 is determined by the collapse temperature of the separator, whereas T3 is determined by the redox reaction between the cathode and anode.
claims.quote: Obviously, the T2 is determined by the collapse temperature of the separator, whereas the T3 is determined by the redox reaction between the cathode and anode.

claims.claim: When separator and electrolyte are removed from the cell (reducing mass by approximately 13%), T2 shifts to 229.4°C and T3 reaches 877.2°C, confirming that ISC does not make major contributions to total heat generation during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: T2=229.4℃, quite near to the triggering temperature, 249.2℃, of the redox reaction. T3=877.2℃ is still high, convincing us that ISC does not make major contributions in the total heat generation during TR. The higher T3 can be explained because the mass (M) of the battery is reduced by removing separator and electrolyte from the cell. The mass is reduced by approximately 13%.

claims.claim: Using only separate kinetic equations for cathode/anode reactions to model thermal runaway behavior fails to fit full-cell TR data, as the predicted T3 will be much lower than measured data due to a mismatch in the heat generation mechanism.
claims.quote: Separate kinetic equations for the cathode/anode reactions have been used to model the TR behavior [7]. However, this methodology fails to establish a model to fit the TR data for the full cell shown in Fig. 2, the predicted T3 will be much lower than the measured data, due to the mismatch in the mechanism of heat generation.

claims.claim: ISC ceases once the temperature rises to 249.2°C, when most of the electrolyte gasifies, thereby cutting off the circuit for internal short circuit.
claims.quote: But the ISC cannot release all the electric energy inside the battery, and will cease once the temperature rises to 249.2℃, when most of the electrolyte gasifies, thereby cutting off the circuit for internal short.

claims.claim: In NMC811|Gr full battery, T1 (start of exothermic side reactions) was located at 130.2 °C, T2 (uncontrollable state threshold at dT/dt = 1 °C/s) was at 206.0 °C, and T3 (maximum temperature) was 791.7 °C.
claims.quote: T1 was located at 130.2 ℃ for the NMC811|Gr full battery, indicating the starting point of exothermic side reactions... T2 of the full battery was located at 206.0 ℃... The battery temperature exponentially increased to the highest temperature of T3, which was 791.7 ℃ for the full battery.

claims.claim: For CaEly, CaAn, and AnEly partial cells, the maximum heat release rate (dT/dt)max was 101.5, 591.0, and 166.5 °C/s respectively, and T3 was 516.1, 920.3, and 476.9 °C respectively.
claims.quote: For the CaEly, CaAn, and AnEly systems, the maximum heat release rate (dT dt−1)max was 101.5, 591.0, and 166.5 ℃ s−1, respectively, while T3 was 516.1, 920.3, and 476.9 ℃, respectively

claims.claim: At T2 = 206.0 °C of the full battery, the heat release rate contributions of CaEly, CaAn, and AnEly reaction systems to the full battery were approximately 90.2%, 7.4%, and 2.4% respectively, identifying CaEly reactions as the thermal runaway triggering reaction.
claims.quote: the heat release ratios of the CaEly, CaAn, and AnEly reaction systems to the full battery at 206.0 ℃ were approximately 90.2%, 7.4%, and 2.4%, respectively... the reactions between Ca and Ely were recognized as the triggering reaction in the NMC811|Gr cell.

claims.claim: Approximately 41.2% of thermally-induced cathode oxygen reacts with EC (pathway O*/O2+EC) generating 16.7% of total heat, while the remaining 58.8% spreads to the lithiated anode (pathway O2+LiC6) generating 64.5% of total heat in the NMC811|Gr cell.
claims.quote: the pathway of O*/O2 +EC consumed 41.2% of the cathode-released oxygen with 16.7% heat generation, whereas the pathway of O2+LiC6 consumed the remaining 58.8% of the cathode-released oxygen with 64.5% heat generation

claims.claim: Using EC-free electrolyte in NMC811|Gr cells increased T2 from 206.0 °C to 235.1 °C and significantly suppressed the self-heating rate, but T3 remained high at 761.3 °C because the O2+LiC6 reaction still provided 82.5% of heat.
claims.quote: The EC-free electrolyte significantly suppressed the self-heating rate with improved T2 (increased from 206.0 ℃ to 235.1 ℃ in Fig. 4d)... T3 of the NMC811 cell_0% EC case kept the high value (761.3 ℃) because the major heat-generating reaction of O2+LiC6 still occurs provided 82.5% heat in the cell

claims.claim: The likelihood of sidewall rupture increases with increasing nail speed, nail diameter, penetrating depth, and state of charge when penetrating from the top cover of the cell.
claims.quote: The likelihood of sidewall rupture increases with the increasing nail speed, nail diameter, penetrating depth and state of charge when penetrating from the top cover of the cell.

claims.claim: The likelihood of sidewall rupture is little affected by penetrating depth and nail diameter when penetrating from the bottom of the cell.
claims.quote: the likelihood of sidewall rupture is little affected by the penetrating depth and nail diameter for penetrating from the bottom of the cell because only one test happened sidewall rupture due to malfunction of the venting disk.

claims.claim: The mean mass loss ratio of cells at 100% SoC exceeded 45% under every test condition, with a maximum of 62.5% when penetrating off-centre from the cell bottom with a penetrating depth of 10 mm.
claims.quote: the mean mass loss ratio of the cell with 100% SoC is greater than 45% under each test condition listed in Table 3, and the maximum of them is as high as 62.5% when penetrating off-centre from the cell bottom and with a penetrating depth of 10 mm

claims.claim: Penetrating from the top cover of the cell is more likely to cause sidewall rupture than penetrating from the bottom of the cell, because the venting disk is damaged and therefore does not function when penetrating from the top cover.
claims.quote: the present results show that the test penetrated from the top cover of the cell is more likely to occur sidewall rupture than that penetrated from the bottom of the cell. The main reason is that the venting disk is damaged and therefore does not work when penetrating from the top cover of the cell.

claims.claim: The tensile strength of the cell casing material monotonously decreases with increasing temperature, and at 800°C drops to 5% of its value at room temperature.
claims.quote: the tensile strength of casing material monotonously decreases with the increasing temperature, and the tensile strength at 800 ◦C drops to 5% of that at room temperature.

claims.claim: The duration of the spark stage during thermal runaway of type 21700 NMC cells at 100% SOC was less than 1.52 s.
claims.quote: The duration of the spark stage was less than 1.52 s.

claims.claim: For 100% SOC type 21700 cells, the first sharp rise in temperature exhibited a rate greater than 15 °C/s, and the shortest time between onset of temperature rise and ignition was 3.3 s.
claims.quote: the first sharp rise in temperature for 100% SOC cells exhibited a rate of temperature rise greater than 15 °C/s; with their time of occurrence during the three tests at 927.4 s, 1038.8 s, and 1066.0 s, respectively. The duration of combustion was found to be greater than 10 s for 100% SOC cells, with the shortest time difference between the onset of temperature rise and ignition being 3.3 s, which is very short for any intervention to reduce cell temperature to prevent TR.

claims.claim: The time to ignition of 30% SOC type 21700 cells was longer than for 100% SOC cells, and the combustion event was also shorter in duration.
claims.quote: The time to ignition of the 30% SOC cells was longer than for 100% SOC cells, with the combustion event also being shorter in duration.

claims.claim: No violent jet fires were observed for 30% SOC type 21700 cells, which was significantly different from 100% SOC cells, and the top cover was not blown away, indicating a much less dynamic combustion process.
claims.quote: No violent jet fires were observed, which was significantly different from the tests for 100% SOC cells... The top cover of the cell was not blown away by the ejected gases, indicating a much less dynamic combustion process for 30% SOC cells when compared to the 100% SOC counterparts.

claims.claim: The estimated peak HRRs for 100% SOC type 21700 NMC cells were 2.88 ± 0.68 kW, 3.08 ± 0.73 kW, and 2.95 ± 0.69 kW for the three tests, which were less than the reported 3.75 ± 0.86 kW for type 18650 LIBs with a similar cathode.
claims.quote: The estimated peak HRRs for tests 1, 2 and 3 are 2.88 ± 0.68 kW, 3.08 ± 0.73 kW, and 2.95 ± 0.69 kW, respectively. These estimates are larger than the previously reported peak HRRs of 1.1 kW [7] and 1.3 kW [10], but less than the value of 3.75 ± 0.86 kW [14] for 18650 LIBs with a similar cathode.

claims.claim: An 18650 Li-ion cell with NCA cathode at 100% SOC produces 7.20 g of gaseous species within 1–10 seconds after reaching the onset temperature of thermal runaway (~160°C).
claims.quote: an 18650 Li-ion cell with NCA cathodes at 100% SOC produces 7.20g of gaseous species within 1-10 sec after the cell reaches the onset temperature of thermal runaway (~160°C)

claims.claim: The Reynolds number for vent flow from an 18650 cell during thermal runaway is in the range of 3000–30000, indicating turbulent flow that decreases in turbulence intensity as gas discharge diminishes over time.
claims.quote: the range of Reynolds number for the vent flow is calculated as: Re_vent ~ 3000 − 30000 ... the vent flow from an 18650 cell during thermal runaway is expected to be turbulent, while the degree of turbulence decreases as the gas discharge diminishes over time

claims.claim: The maximum internal gas pressure during thermal runaway increases with SOC, reaching 4.25 bar, 1.71 bar, and 0.20 bar at 100%, 50%, and 25% SOC, respectively.
claims.quote: The maximum internal gas pressures are 4.25 bar, 1.71 bar and 0.20 bar at 100%, 50% and 25% SOCs, respectively, indicating that the maximum gas pressure build-up during the thermal runaway increases with SOC.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the contribution of gas-phase reactions to total heat generation is 3 times larger than that of thermal abuse reactions occurring within the cell components.
claims.quote: At 100% SOC, the contribution of the gas-phase reactions to the total heat generation is 3 times larger than that of the thermal abuse reactions taking place among the cell components.

claims.claim: The pressure required to split a 220 µm thick mild steel casing was found to be less than 2 bar at temperatures above 600°C, making pressure-induced side-wall breach a high risk when internal pressure reaches 4.25 bar.
claims.quote: The pressure required to split a 220 µm thick mild steel casing was found to be < 2 bar at temperatures > 600 °C [3]. Thus, the pressure of 4.25 bar predicted to build up within the cells from our models, indicates that some cell designs are likely to undergo pressure-induced side-wall breach during thermal runaway

claims.claim: For the KULR 21700-K500, nail penetration trigger resulted in an average of 12.3% higher heat output compared to Heater (ISC) and 16.8% higher compared to Heater (Non-ISC).
claims.quote: The KULR 21700-K500 appeared to have an average of 12.3 % higher heat output for Nail Penetration vs. Heater (ISC) and a 16.8 % higher for Nail Penetration vs. heater (Non-ISC).

claims.claim: For the Saft 33600-VES16, nail penetration trigger resulted in an average of 18.1% higher heat output compared to Heater (ISC) and 28.4% higher compared to Heater (Non-ISC).
claims.quote: The Saft 33600-VES16 had an average of 18.1 % higher heat output for Nail Penetration trigger vs. Heater (ISC) trigger and 28.4 % higher for Nail Penetration trigger vs. Heater (Non-ISC) trigger.

claims.claim: The difference in heat output between nail penetration and thermal abuse increases with increasing cell diameter, hypothesized to be caused by nail penetration immediately initiating thermal runaway across every punctured layer while thermal abuse causes slower radial propagation from layer to layer.
claims.quote: These results quantitatively show that the difference in heat output between nail and thermal abuse increases with increasing diameter of cell. This is hypothesized to be caused by an increase in the rate of thermal runaway propagation for nail penetration tests for larger diameter cells. This is due to the nail immediately initiating thermal runaway across every layer that it punctures, from the outer layer near the casing, to the inner layer at the center of the cell. Whereas during thermal abuse the radial propagation occurs in slower steps from layer to layer.

claims.claim: The KULR 18650-K330 did not show significant variation in total heat output as a function of trigger method, suggesting worst-case scenario heat output is achievable in assembly level testing regardless of trigger method for this cell format.
claims.quote: There was not a significant difference in total heat output as a function of trigger method for the KULR 18650-K330... Additionally, the KULR 18650-K330 results suggest that worst-case scenario heat output is achievable in assembly level testing regardless of the utilized trigger method.

claims.claim: Nail penetration tended to result in the highest fraction of energy released through the cell body compared to other trigger methods, possibly because the nail traps electrode winding material inside the cell casing forcing decomposition reactions to occur within the casing.
claims.quote: Other than for the KULR 21700-K500, nail penetration tended to result in the highest fraction of energy being released through the cell body. This is possibly because the nail traps a portion of the electrode winding assembly inside of the cell casing, which forces the decomposition reactions to occur within the casing, which in turn means that a higher fraction of the total energy will be released through the cell body.

claims.claim: The average threshold valve opening pressure for the safety vent in the tested 18650 NCA cells was approximately 1.69 MPa.
claims.quote: The averaged repeated testing results indicated that the threshold valve opening pressure vP was ~1.69 MPa (Table 3).

claims.claim: The average burst pressure of the 18650 NCA cell shell when the safety valve was sealed was approximately 6.78 MPa.
claims.quote: The average burst pressure fP was measured as ~6.78 MPa (Table 3) (define the facture pressure as fP).

claims.claim: Adding a tight-fitting CFRP sleeve around the cell effectively prevented sidewall rupture during thermal runaway, with no sidewall rupture observed in any of the five tested samples with CFRP.
claims.quote: The TR tests of samples with the CFRP layer suggest that the cell sidewall rupture can be effectively limited by the CFRP layer: there was no sidewall rupture observed.

claims.claim: The simulated fracture pressure of the battery shell with a CFRP sleeve (~7.07 MPa) was lower than the simulated fracture pressure of the shell without a CFRP sleeve (~8.79 MPa).
claims.quote: The simulation results indicate that fP of the battery shell with CFRP is ~7.07 MPa, which is smaller than fP of the battery shell without CFRP (8.79 MPa).

claims.claim: When shell thickness decreases below 0.08 mm, the deformation mode changes from an axial dent to a severe 'X' shape, because decreasing thickness reduces the in-plane critical buckling force.
claims.quote: For shells thickness larger than 0.08 mm, the deformation is a kind of axial direction dent. However, when the thickness decreases to 0.04 mm, the deformation changes to an 'X' shape and becomes very severe. This is because when thickness decreases, the in-plane critical force for buckling of the shell decreases.

claims.claim: Under uniform temperature distribution during thermal runaway, shell fracture tends to occur at the edge of the battery end cap due to stress concentration from abrupt geometry change, whereas under laterally concentrated temperature distribution, fracture originates at the point of highest heat concentration and propagates outward.
claims.quote: The strain distribution is uniform due to the uniform temperature distribution, and the rupture occurred near the edge of the cap with the maximum stress concentration due to the abrupt geometry shape change... For the Type II uneven distribution case, the fracture originates at the point of highest heat concentration and then propagates to the surrounding area, representing the side rupture scenario.

claims.claim: The axial dent observed on cells with CFRP sleeves during thermal runaway is caused by uneven temperature distribution: high-temperature areas soften the steel shell, producing unrecoverable plastic strain, while the CFRP layer prevents outward expansion, forcing the shell to bend inward.
claims.quote: When the temperature increased, plastic strain and deformation occurred in the high-temperature area... The plastic strain and deformation are unrecoverable. However, the CFRP layer prevent the outward expansion therefore the steel stretching had to bend inward. The axial dent was finally produced. In this case, the uneven temperature distribution further leads to the occurrence of an axial dent.

claims.claim: Lithium-ion batteries (NMC811/SiC, 21700 and 18650 formats) reached venting gas temperatures exceeding 1370°C during thermal runaway, which is the measurement limit of the thermocouple used.
claims.quote: During testing, the venting gas temperature of LIBs exceeded 1000°C and, in some tests, reached values up to 1370°C, which is the measurement limit of the thermocouple's measurement capability. However, the actual venting gas temperature may have been even higher.

claims.claim: Sodium-ion batteries (NaMn1/3Fe1/3Ni1/3O2 cathode, 18650 format) exhibited peak venting gas temperatures not exceeding 350°C during thermal runaway, with no gas ignition observed.
claims.quote: The measured peak venting gas temperatures of the 18 650 SIBs were significantly lower, with values not exceeding 350°C. The lower temperature measured compared to LIBs may be connected to the lower energy stored in the investigated SIBs... Our measurement results are supported by videos, where it can be observed that there was no ignition or fire in tested SIBs, leading to measuring lower gas temperatures compared to both LIB types.

claims.claim: Peak mass flow rate during thermal runaway reached 2000 g/s for 18650 LIBs and 1500 g/s for 21700 LIBs, but these peak values lasted only several milliseconds.
claims.quote: The peak values of the mass flow rate were even higher - 1500 g s−1 for 21 700 LIBs and 2000 g s−1 for 18 650 LIBs but lasting only several milliseconds.

claims.claim: The average total mass loss during thermal runaway was 77% for 21700 LIBs and 75% for 18650 LIBs, compared to only 55% for 18650 SIBs.
claims.quote: The average total mass loss for 21 700 LIBs is 77 % and for 18 650 LIBs it is 75 %... Compared to both LIBs, the average total mass loss of the investigated SIBs in 18 650 format is only 55 %.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway start temperature of investigated 18650 SIBs was on average 10°C to 15°C higher than that of investigated 18650 LIBs, suggesting SIBs have a larger safety window before TR onset.
claims.quote: Compared to LIBs, the TR start temperature of investigated SIBs is, on average, 10°C to 15°C higher, and, thus, the TR occurred later despite the same heating rate, indicating that investigated SIBs could be more thermally stable compared to investigated LIBs.

claims.claim: The venting gas velocity of LIBs (both 21700 and 18650 formats) exceeded 100 m/s at the beginning of thermal runaway and decreased below 50 m/s after 200 ms, exhibiting an exponential decay over time.
claims.quote: The high velocities exceeding 100 m s−1 at the beginning of TR can be attributed to the gas ignition. The venting gas velocity decreases after 200 ms, falling below 50 m s−1 in both LIB formats and further drops below the 25 m s−1 value after 500 ms since the beginning of the optically visible TR event.

claims.claim: The absence of gas ignition in investigated SIBs precluded optical estimation of venting gas velocity and mass flow rate calculation, representing a limitation of the methodology for SIB characterization.
claims.quote: The mass flow rate for the 18 650 SIBs could not be estimated due to the absence of ignition and the venting gas velocity values... The investigation of SIBs in the 18 650 format using optical velocity estimation was impossible since the vented gas did not ignite in any of the three tested cells.

claims.claim: Under heat-penetration coupled stimulations at 100°C, the average temperature rise rate of NCM811 cells increases from 1.992°C/s at 0% SOC to 93.033°C/s at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: at critical 100℃, as the SOC increases from 0% to 100%, the average temperature rise rate of the cell sharply increases from 1.992°C/s to 93.033°C/s

claims.claim: Under heat-penetration coupled stimulations at 100°C, the maximum cell temperature increases from 125.9°C at 0% SOC to 652.9°C at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature of cell increases from 125.9°C to 652.9°C, and the mass loss increases from 3.332 g to 31.180 g

claims.claim: The critical SOC for flame generation in NCM811 cells subjected to nail penetration at 100°C is 25%; cells with 0%, 10%, 15%, and 20% SOC did not produce a flame under these conditions.
claims.quote: Because the 0%, 10%, 15%, and 20% SOC cell TR did not produce a flame, only the flame temperature and pattern of the 25%, 50%, 75% and 100% SOC cell are analyzed in this paper. Therefore, the critical SOC leading to flame generation under heat-penetration coupled stimulations is 25% for NCM811 cell.

claims.claim: Nail penetration of a 25% SOC NCM811 cell at 100°C produces a jet flame and elevates the maximum temperature by 76.1°C (18% higher) compared to penetration at room temperature, where no jet flame is produced.
claims.quote: penetration at room temperature, the 25% SOC NCM811 cell will not produce a jet fire, whereas penetration at 100°C, the 25% SOC NCM811 cell still produces jet fire, penetration at 100°C elevates the fire risk in contrast to room temperature. And the maximum temperature of cell was elevated by 76.1°C, which was 18% higher than that of penetration at room temperature.

claims.claim: Under heat-penetration coupled stimulations at 100°C, the TR flame temperature increases from 437.8°C at 25% SOC to 918.5°C at 100% SOC, and the maximum flame area increases from 2419.14 cm² to 9235.42 cm².
claims.quote: the flame temperature increases from 437.8°C to 918.5°C, the flame area rise ratio reaching 281.77%... the maximum area of the cell jet flame gradually increases as the SOC increases. From 25% SOC to 100% SOC, the maximum flame area was raised from 2419.14 cm2 to 9235.42 cm2

claims.claim: SEI decomposition generally occurs at about 100°C, at which point the SEI starts to decompose and regenerate, constantly releasing heat and increasing the battery's internal temperature.
claims.quote: decomposition, which generally occurs at about 100 ◦C. At this moment, the SEI starts to decompose and regenerate, constantly releasing heat and increasing the battery's internal temperature.

claims.claim: The separator, normally manufactured with polypropylene or polyethylene, melts when the battery temperature achieves 120–130°C, causing a short circuit due to the loss of protection between the anode and cathode.
claims.quote: the separator, normally manufactured with polypropylene or polyethylene, is melted when the battery temperature achieves 120–130 ◦C. A short circuit occurs due to the inexistence of protection between the anode and cathode, producing more heat according to Joule's law.

claims.claim: NMC batteries showed a maximum accumulated heat release of approximately 28 kJ and a maximum temperature rise rate of approximately 3100°C/min, while pristine LFP batteries showed a maximum accumulated heat release of approximately 5.5 kJ and a maximum temperature rise rate of approximately 1.1°C/min.
claims.quote: the maximum accumulated heat released and temperature rise rate on average were 28 kJ and 3100 ◦C/min, respectively. These values are significantly higher than those obtained for LFP batteries

claims.claim: Pristine LFP batteries did not exhibit sufficient self-sustaining exothermic reactions to induce thermal runaway during external heating tests, with maximum temperature remaining below 315°C.
claims.quote: LFP batteries did not exhibit a sufficient self-sustaining chemical reaction to induce thermal runaway during the heating tests. For this reason, the onset temperature and crucial temperature have no values. The maximum temperature was lower than 315 ◦C

claims.claim: For aged batteries, SEI growth consumes lithium and electrolyte in the anode, reducing the quantity of both components and thereby decreasing heat released during the anode-electrolyte reaction; compared to pristine conditions, heat released was lower by 12.3% for NMC and 68.9% for LFP when aged at 50°C.
claims.quote: The main outcome of this process is the reduction of the cycled lithium and the electrolyte present in the anode. The SEI growth increases the thickness using both elements, reducing the battery's capacity. Considering this process, the only influence that can occur in the heat release calculator is in Reaction (5) and Reaction (7). Both elements are directly in contact during Reaction (5), and less quantity results in less heat release. Compared with the Pristine case, Fig. 9, the heat released is lower, 12.3% for the NMC and 68.9% for the LFP.

claims.claim: The thermal safety of the LFP battery relative to NMC is attributed to the endothermic behavior of cathode decomposition and less oxygen released during this process; LFP releases approximately half the oxygen released by NMC cathodes, resulting in about 11.5 kJ less heat from electrolyte oxidation.
claims.quote: the oxygen released during this reaction is lower than the NMC battery. It resulted in a lower heat released in Reaction (4), a difference of about 11.5 kJ higher for the NMC chemistry.

claims.claim: Batteries aged at 50°C ambient temperature showed a lower maximum temperature and lower temperature rise rate during thermal runaway compared to pristine NMC batteries, but the thermal runaway process was triggered at a lower onset temperature and lower crucial temperature.
claims.quote: The average maximum temperature and temperature rise rate achieved were lower than the pristine NMC batteries. In addition, the thermal runaway process was triggered before, as illustrated by the lower onset temperature and crucial temperature.

claims.claim: NEPCM with 6% SWCNT increases the melting temperature distribution by up to 15.27% compared to pure PCM setups.
claims.quote: NEPCM with 6 % SWCNT increases the melting temperature distribution by up to 15.27 % compared to pure PCM setups

claims.claim: NEPCM with 6% SWCNT enhances the liquid fraction by up to 66.54% compared to pure PCM under similar conditions.
claims.quote: enhances the liquid fraction by up to 66.54 % under similar conditions

claims.claim: SWCNT nanoparticles demonstrate superior enhancement in thermal conductivity compared to alumina, leading to more effective heat absorption and dissipation.
claims.quote: The inclusion of SWCNT demonstrates a superior enhancement in thermal conductivity compared to alumina, leading to more effective heat absorption and dissipation.

claims.claim: PCM1, positioned adjacent to the battery, exhibits an immediate increase in temperature and melting rate, significantly outperforming PCM2 in thermal regulation.
claims.quote: PCM1, positioned adjacent to the battery, exhibits an immediate increase in temperature and melting rate, significantly outperforming PCM2 in thermal regulation.

claims.claim: The average melting fraction of PCM1 (RT82) is 55.35% higher than that of PCM2 (RT27), attributed to its proximity to the heat source.
claims.quote: the average melting fraction of PCM 1 (RT82) is observed to be 55.35 % higher than that of PCM 2 (RT27), underscoring the enhanced thermal performance facilitated by the proximity to the heat source

claims.claim: The temperature rise of a 3.5 Ah NCM 18650-type cell increases as discharge current increases, with a maximal temperature rise of 14.8°C at 2C discharge rate in a chamber set to 20°C.
claims.quote: the temperature rise of the cell increases as the current increases, especially at the end of discharge. The temperature rises gently and slightly below the 1C discharge current rate but rapidly and drastically above 2C, for which the maximal temperature rise reaches 14.8C at 2C.

claims.claim: At discharge current rates below 1C, the temperature rise of the cell is sufficiently small (below 3°C when SOC is as low as 0.2 at 1C) that estimated parameters can be treated as true values for lookup table preparation.
claims.quote: the temperature rise of 1C is below 3C when the SOC is as low as 0.2; therefore, the estimated results of discharge current rate (<1C) at different temperatures can be considered the true values of the corresponding temperature and prepared for lookup tables.

claims.claim: Parameters εe,n, εe,p, and εe,s are insensitive to variation, as output voltage curves remain anastomotic even when the input parameter is decreased to 0.04, while Ds,n, Ds,p, εs,n, and εs,p are sensitive parameters whose slight changes greatly influence model output, especially at the end of discharge.
claims.quote: Even when the input parameter is sharply decreased to 0.04, the output curves are all anastomotic, which indicates that the three parameters are insensitive... the output curves vary greatly, especially at the end of discharge, which demonstrates that the four parameters are sensitive parameters and that slight changes in their values can greatly influence the model's fidelity.

claims.claim: In the negative electrode, both Ds,n and εs,n increase with increasing temperature, but only Ds,n increases appreciably with increasing current.
claims.quote: In the negative electrode, Ds,n (Figure 5a) and εs,n (Figure 5e) both increase with increasing temperature, but only Ds,n increases appreciably with increasing current.

claims.claim: The maximum estimated voltage error appears at the end of discharge (~0.25 SOC) due to strong nonlinear battery characteristics at extreme SOC, while voltage relative errors are within 0.5% for intermediate SOC across temperatures from 10°C to 40°C at 0.2C.
claims.quote: the maximum estimated error appears at the end of discharge (~0.25 SOC), generally because the battery exhibits strong nonlinear characteristics at extreme SOC universally... the voltage relative errors are essentially within 0.5%... the vehicle batteries mainly operate in the middle of the SOC, which indicates that the proposed method has high fidelity and strong robustness to temperature change.

claims.claim: The optimal operating temperature range of a Lithium-ion battery is 15°C to 35°C.
claims.quote: It is stated that the optimal temperature range of LiB is 15°C to 35°C

claims.claim: At low temperatures, the charge transfer resistance of a Lithium-ion battery is three times more than the resistance at room temperature.
claims.quote: In this situation, the charge transfer resistance will be three times more than the resistance at room temperature.

claims.claim: Heat generation in a Lithium-ion battery occurs in a sequential order: capacity loss and decomposition at 60°C, exothermic reaction affecting electrode material at 100°C, separator melting at 130°C, anode and cathode decomposition at 240°C, and at 800°C increased internal pressure leads to emission of harmful gas, smoke, fire, and explosion.
claims.quote: The heat generation is happening in the following order: (i) At 60°C - Capacity loss and decomposition, (ii) Exothermic reaction affects electrode material at 100°C, (iii) Separator melting at 130°C, (iv) Anode and cathode decomposition at 240°C, and (v) When the temperature reaches 800°C, it increases internal pressure and leads to emission of harmful gas, smokes, fire and unexpected explosion

claims.claim: Under the same pumping power, oil cooling provides 1.5 to 3 times better cooling response than water cooling.
claims.quote: it is understood that under the same pumping power (KW) oil cooling provides 1.5 to 3 times better response than water

claims.claim: During charging conditions, the maximum temperature was 37.5°C without cooling, 35.6°C with water cooling, and 34.7°C with oil cooling; during discharging conditions, the maximum temperature was 36.7°C without cooling, 35.3°C with water cooling, and 34.2°C with oil cooling.
claims.quote: During the charging conditions, the maximum temperature was maintained at 37.5°C, 35.6°C, and 34.7°C for no-cooling, air-cooling, and liquid-cooling, respectively. Similarly, the maximum temperature was maintained at 36.7°C, 35.3°C, and 34.2°C for no-cooling, air-cooling, and liquid-cooling, respectively, during discharging conditions.

claims.claim: When short-circuit resistance was 1 or 3 μΩ, the battery temperature rapidly exceeded 498 K at 21 s after discharge and voltage quickly decreased to near-zero within 40 s.
claims.quote: When the short circuit resistance was 1 and 3 μΩ, heat generated by the short-circuit resistance, when a large current was passed through rapidly, accumulated and could not be quickly dissipated to the surroundings. As a result, the temperature of the battery rapidly exceeded 498 K at 21 s after discharge and correspondingly the voltage quickly decreased to near-zero within 40 s

claims.claim: When short-circuit resistance was 30 μΩ, the local maximum temperature did not rise until a 70-s delay and reached only 430 K, after which temperature rapidly decreased because heat dissipation rate exceeded heat generation rate.
claims.quote: when the short-circuit resistance increased by one order of magnitude, for example at 30 μΩ as shown in Fig. 6, voltage showed a gentle decrease process for about 50 s and then began a rapid decrease lasting for 30 s. After 80 s, the voltage began to decrease slowly to zero. The concentrated release of energy was much slower than that in the two aforementioned conditions, so that Ohmic heat generated from short-circuit current had relatively enough time to be dissipated to the surroundings and environment, and therefore, the temperature of the local short-circuit location did not rise to the maximum (430 K) until a 70-s delay. After that, owing to the rate of heat generation being smaller than the rate of heat dissipation, the temperature rapidly decreased from the maximum.

claims.claim: Under ISC conditions with discharge rate 1C and ISC resistance 1 μΩ, the maximum temperature at the bottom-center location C was 413 K, at top-center location A was 411 K, and at center location B was 409 K within 5 s.
claims.quote: When the short circuit appeared at central location B, the temperature rose at the slowest rate to the maximum temperature of 409 K, followed by that at A (the maximum temperature being 411 K), while the temperature rise rate in the bottom location C was the fastest (the maximum temperature being 413 K).

claims.claim: Five seconds after ISC occurrence, the maximum temperature of the battery showed a linear decreasing trend with increasing discharge rate, and this decreasing trend diminished with increasing short-circuit resistance.
claims.quote: Five seconds after the occurrence of a short circuit, the maximum temperature of the battery showed a linear decreasing trend with an increasing discharge rate and such a trend tended to diminish with increasing short-circuit resistance.

claims.claim: Under ISC conditions with short-circuit resistance 0.5 μΩ, the electrochemical reaction heat generation accounted for about 80% of total heat generation, Ohmic heat from short-circuit current accounted for about 20%, and Ohmic volumetric heat generation was no more than 1% of the total.
claims.quote: the heat generation rate of the electrochemical reaction accounts for about 80%, and Ohmic heat generation from the short-circuit current accounts for about 20%, of the total heat generation, however, the proportion of Ohmic volumetric heat generation is no more than 1% overall.

claims.claim: At 0°C, the capacity retention rate of SIBs is 13.93% higher than that of LIBs at 0.5C discharge rate.
claims.quote: at 0°C, the capacity retention rates further decline to 90.20 % for SIBs and 76.26 % for LIBs

claims.claim: Under maximum overcharge conditions (15%), SIBs experienced thermal runaway reaching a peak temperature of 273.9°C, while LIBs did not exhibit thermal runaway under identical heating conditions.
claims.quote: the data reveal that under identical heating conditions, the SIB experienced thermal runaway, whereas the LIB did not... the peak temperature at T2 reached 273.9°C

claims.claim: After 200 cycles at 3C discharge rate, the capacity retention rate of SIBs is 15.81% lower than that of LIBs.
claims.quote: At discharge rates of 0.5 C, 1 C, 1.5 C, 2 C, and 3 C, the capacity retention rates for SIBs are 97.51 %, 93.45 %, 90.29 %, 85.44 %, and 79.28 %, respectively. In comparison, LIBs exhibit capacity retention rates of 97.63 %, 96.66 %, 96.18 %, 94.53 %, and 95.09 %

claims.claim: At 15% overcharge level, the residual capacity of SIBs was 67.72%, which is 33.35% lower than that of LIBs, while LIBs maintained 99.07% residual capacity.
claims.quote: For SIBs, the residual capacities of the NB and OB at 5 %, 10 %, and 15 % overcharging were 99.70 %, 97.65 %, 92.08 %, and 67.72 %, respectively... the capacity retention rate of SIBs is 33.35 % lower than that of LIBs

claims.claim: Under normal operating conditions at maximum discharge rate (3C), SIBs generate less surface heat than LIBs by 2.683°C, but under maximum overcharging (15%), SIBs produce more surface heat than LIBs by 1.53°C.
claims.quote: Under normal working conditions at the maximum discharge rate (3 C), SIBs generate less surface heat than LIBs, with a difference of 2.683°C. However, under the maximum overcharging level (15 %) in abusive conditions, SIBs produce more surface heat than LIBs, with a difference of 1.53°C

claims.claim: The bottom vent of the 21,700 M50 cylindrical cell with NMC811 cathode undergoes mechanical rupture when internal gas pressure reaches 2 MPa.
claims.quote: Gulsoy et al. [13] experimentally determined that the bottom vent of the 21,700 M50 cylindrical cell with Li(Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1)O2 (NMC811) cathode undergoes mechanical rupture when the internal gas pressure reaches 2 MPa.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of the 21,700 M50 NMC811 cell, gas composition includes hydrogen at 25.3%, carbon dioxide at 38.2%, carbon monoxide at 16.2%, methane at 17.6%, and ethane at 2.7%.
claims.quote: Abbott et al. [14] analyzed the gas composition generated during TR of the same cell, with hydrogen accounting for 25.3%, carbon dioxide at 38.2%, carbon monoxide at 16.2%, methane at 17.6%, and ethane at 2.7%.

claims.claim: The onset temperature (Tonset) at which temperature rise rate increases significantly and consistently corresponds to 105 °C, representing departure from quasi-equilibrium transitioning from stable self-heating (Stage II) to violently accelerated heat release (Stage III).
claims.quote: Tonset is the initial temperature at which the temperature rise rate increases significantly and consistently, corresponding to 105 °C in this study. This temperature point represents the departure of the system from quasi-equilibrium, transitioning from a relatively stable self-heating (Stage II) to violently accelerated heat release (Stage III).

claims.claim: The thermal runaway onset temperature TTR, defined by a temperature rise rate of 1 °C/s, was observed at 143 °C for the 21,700 M50 NMC811 cell under EV-ARC test.
claims.quote: TTR refers to the onset temperature of TR, defined by a temperature rise rate of 1 °C/s, and is observed at 143 °C.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature and maximum thermal rate of the 21,700 M50 LIB cell during the thermal runaway process reached 728 °C and 7688 °C/min, respectively, under EV-ARC test.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature and maximum thermal rate of the LIB cell during the TR process reached 728 °C and 7688 °C/min, respectively.

claims.claim: In NCM811|AG battery with 1M LiPF6 EC/EMC electrolyte, the three characteristic temperatures during ARC test were T1=65°C (onset of self-heating), T2=218°C (thermal runaway trigger, defined when temperature rise rate reached 1°C/s), and T3=750°C (maximum temperature).
claims.quote: In this study, the three characteristic temperatures were located at 65℃ (T1), 218℃ (T2) and 750℃ (T3), respectively.

claims.claim: For the Ca+Ely sample (charged NCM811 cathode mixed with electrolyte), two DSC exothermic peaks appeared at 215°C and 230°C, and the reaction between cathode and electrolyte was identified as the thermal runaway trigger based on the time order of reactions.
claims.quote: As shown in Fig. 1a, for the Ca +Ely sample, two peaks appeared at 215℃ and 230℃. And for the Ca +Ely +An sample, a sharp exothermic peak was also detected at 215℃. Based on the time order of the reactions, the reaction between cathode and electrolyte was identified as the thermal runaway trigger.

claims.claim: For dry charged NCM811 cathode, the oxygen gas release peak initiated at approximately 240°C; however, when electrolyte was added, a sharp oxygen gas peak centered at approximately 230°C was detected, indicating that electrolyte presence shifted and sharpened the oxygen release.
claims.quote: For the dry cathode, the peak initiated at ca. 240oC, which was delayed to the phase transformation. However, the oxygen gas release behavior of the cathode with an electrolyte addition was quite different and a sharp oxygen gas peak centered at ~230oC was detected.

claims.claim: When TPFPB (an anion receptor) was added to the electrolyte in NCM811 full batteries, T2 decreased to 198°C (20°C lower than for NCM811 without TPFPB), indicating that TPFPB addition made the battery more susceptible to thermal runaway onset, attributed to TPFPB reducing oxygen species to oxygen gas at lower temperature.
claims.quote: The key temperature, T2, was 198 oC for B-NCM811, 20 oC lower than for NCM811, indicating that the battery was more likely to thermal runaway after TPFPB added. By connecting the abovementioned profiles of oxygen release for the charged NCM811 cathodes without and in the presence of TPFPB (Fig. 3e), the lower T2 could be mainly attributed to the reducibility of TPFPB, which makes oxygen gas release at lower temperature.

claims.claim: In the presence of electrolyte, the lattice c of charged NCM811 cathode exhibits moderate evolution and strain is released spontaneously by cation migration, phase transformation and oxygen release, whereas the dry cathode can endure much greater lattice strain, meaning electrolyte accelerates phase transition and oxygen release.
claims.quote: the dry cathode can withhold more c lattice expansion than it in the presence of electrolyte and TPFPB, thereby the oxygen release becomes more difficult... the strain in the presence of electrolyte is released spontaneously by cation migration, phase transformation and oxygen release. Therefore, the results show that electrolyte has a negative impact on the phase structure stability of cathode, which could accelerate phase transition and oxygen release.

claims.claim: Data collected at C/30 rate is described as suitable for open circuit voltage characterization of Li-ion batteries.
claims.quote: Such low current rate data is suitable for open circuit voltage characterization.

claims.claim: OCV characterization data were collected over a temperature range from −25 °C to 50 °C.
claims.quote: Data were collected over a wide range of temperatures from −25 ◦C to 50 ◦C.

claims.claim: Each battery cell was fully charged at room temperature (25 °C) at C/30 rate until the terminal voltage reached 4.2 V before OCV testing.
claims.quote: The battery was fully charged at room temperature ( 25 ◦C ) at C/30 rate until the terminal voltage reached 4.2 V.

claims.claim: During OCV characterization, the battery was discharged at C/30 rate until the terminal voltage reached 3 V, followed by a 30-second rest, then charged at C/30 rate until the terminal voltage reached 4.2 V.
claims.quote: The battery is discharged at C/30 rate until the terminal voltage reached 3 V. A 30 seconds rest was given. The battery is charged at C/30 rate until the terminal voltage reached 4.2 V.

claims.claim: Batteries with unusually low charge/discharge capacities were classified as dead and excluded from further analysis.
claims.quote: the charge/discharge capacities of some of the batteries were found to be unusually low; these batteries were considered dead and were removed from further analysis as well.

claims.claim: The initial DCIR values of 18650 LIBs with Li(Ni0.5Co0.2Mn0.3)O2 cathode at 92% SOC, 100% SOC, 108% SOC, and 116% SOC were 45 mΩ, 50 mΩ, 57 mΩ, and 69 mΩ, respectively, increasing with SOC.
claims.quote: The initial DCIR values of LIBs for 92% SOC, 100% SOC, 108% SOC and 116% SOC were 45 mΩ, 50 mΩ, 57 mΩ and 69 mΩ, respectively.

claims.claim: After 1000 cycles at 0.5 C, capacity retention of LIBs with 108% SOC and 116% SOC decreased to 50.1% and 32.4%, respectively, while LIBs at 92% SOC and 100% SOC retained 70.5% and 69.9% capacity.
claims.quote: The capacity retention of LIBs with 108% SOC and 116% SOC after 1000 cycles decrease to 50.1% and 32.4%, respectively. It can be found that there exits little difference between the capacity retention of LIBs with 92% SOC and 100% SOC after 1000 cycles (70.5% for 92% SOC and 69.9% for 100% SOC).

claims.claim: Under adiabatic conditions, the onset temperature of exothermic reaction (TO) for LIBs increases with increasing SOC: 64.59°C at 92% SOC, 68.02°C at 100% SOC, 71.23°C at 108% SOC, and 75.01°C at 116% SOC, indicating decreasing thermal stability with higher SOC.
claims.quote: Under adiabatic environment, TO of LIB are found to be 64.59 ◦C, 69.02 ◦C, 71.23 ◦C and 75.01 ◦C for 92% SOC, 100% SOC, 108% SOC and 116% SOC, respectively. TO of LIB increases with the increase of SOC, which reflecting the thermal stability of LIB decrease.

claims.claim: Under adiabatic conditions, the maximum exothermic temperature (TMax) increases from 560.58°C at 92% SOC to 780.02°C at 116% SOC, indicating more energy stored in higher SOC batteries.
claims.quote: With the increase of SOC, TMax is raised from 560.58 ◦C to 780.02 ◦C and thereby indicating that there is more power in the overcharged battery as TMax is related to the energy stored in LIB.

claims.claim: When LIB is charged and discharged under adiabatic conditions, the surface temperature of LIB at 116% SOC is approximately 10.03°C higher than that of LIB at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: Surface temperature of LIB with 116% SOC is about 10.03 ◦C higher than that of LIB with 100% SOC.

claims.claim: The data-driven MMCSA approach achieves a maximum RMSE of 9 mV for full cell voltage under constant current (2C) discharging, which is 17.9% of that of the experimental identification approach (50.1 mV).
claims.quote: the data-driven approach achieves a maximum root mean square error of 9 mV and 12.7 mV for the full cell voltage under constant current discharging and real-world driving cycles, respectively, which is only 17.9% and 42.9% of that of the experimental identiﬁcation approach.

claims.claim: The capacity error of the data-driven MMCSA method is 3.4 mAh, which is only 4.6% of the capacity error from the invasive experimental identification method (74.2 mAh).
claims.quote: The capacity error of the data-driven method is 3.4 mAh, which is only 4.6% of that of the experimental identiﬁcation.

claims.claim: For a high-energy NMC/graphite cell, electrolyte- and separator-related parameters have relatively low sensitivity, but this relationship does not hold for high-power cells.
claims.quote: the cell being analyzed in this work is a high-energy cell, the electrolyte- and separator-related parameters have relatively low sensitivity, which will not be the same for the high-power cells.

claims.claim: A flat OCV curve in LFP and LTO cells reduces the sensitivity of capacity-related parameters significantly compared to NMC/graphite cells.
claims.quote: the ﬂat OCV curve in LFP and LTO cells will reduce the sensitivity of the capacity-related parameters signiﬁcantly.

claims.claim: Stronger weighting of the pulse profile during MMCSA training resulted in significant overfitting, shown by high voltage error under the WLTP 2 validation profile, whereas stronger weighting of the 2C discharge profile led to lower overfitting and continuously reduced validation voltage MSE.
claims.quote: a stronger weighting of the 2C discharge proﬁle in both steps led to signiﬁcantly lower overﬁtting of the training data and thus continuously reduced the voltage MSE of the validation proﬁle with the iterations. In contrast, a stronger weighting of the pulse proﬁle resulted in signiﬁcant overﬁtting, which is shown by the high voltage error under the WLTP 2 proﬁle.

claims.claim: The overall capacity degradation hierarchy across all five protocols was: 4C CC–CV 15 min > 4C CC–CV 60 %SOC > 3C CC > 2C CC > 4C CC.
claims.quote: The overall degradation hierarchy was: 4C CC–CV 15 min > 4C CC–CV 60 %SOC > 3C CC > 2C CC > 4C CC.

claims.claim: Under 4C CC–CV 15 min cycling, capacity fade is minimal during early cycles but degrades rapidly in later cycles, reaching 80% SOH first among all protocols tested.
claims.quote: cells charged at 4C CC–CV 15 min exhibited minimal fade during early cycles but degraded rapidly in later cycles, reaching 80 % SOH first.

claims.claim: Under 3C CC and 4C CC cycling, impedance and capacity degrade rapidly at first but then plateau or slow in the mid-to-late stages as the SEI stabilizes.
claims.quote: At 3C CC and 4C CC, impedance and capacity degrade rapidly at first but then plateau or slow in the mid-to-late stages as the SEI stabilizes.

claims.claim: Under 2C CC cycling, impedance and capacity loss worsen continuously without stabilization.
claims.quote: Under 2C CC cycling, impedance and capacity loss worsen continuously.

claims.claim: Peak cell temperature stays below 50°C across all five charging protocols tested.
claims.quote: the peak cell temperature stays below 50 ◦C across all charging protocols.

claims.claim: The heating coil method triggered thermal runaway after 225 s of heating, which was 34.00 s and 57.79 s faster than the heating rod and heating plate methods, respectively, under identical 265 W heating power.
claims.quote: thermal runaway is triggered after 225 s of heating with the coil, reaching 140.07 C higher than the temperatures triggered by the rod and plate, respectively. Triggering times are reduced by 34.00 s and 57.79 s, respectively.

claims.claim: Under the heating coil method, the battery peak temperature after thermal runaway was the lowest at 326.03 °C, while the heating rod produced the highest peak temperature at 508.67 °C, and the heating plate produced an intermediate peak temperature of 460.35 °C.
claims.quote: The peak temperature data indicate the following order from lowest to highest: heating coil (326.03 C), heating plate (460.35 C), and heating rod (508.67 C).

claims.claim: Under heating coil conditions, the peak chamber pressure was the highest at 430.43 kPa despite the lowest ambient temperature among the three methods, indicating that higher gas generation rate was the primary driver of pressure increase.
claims.quote: under heating coil conditions, the ambient temperature is the lowest, yet the corresponding peak pressure is the highest at 430.43 kPa. This indicates that a higher gas generation rate is the primary driver of the pressure increase.

claims.claim: The heating coil method produced the highest peak gas generation at 7.72 mol, significantly exceeding the heating plate and rod methods, with all methods stabilizing between 1.7 and 2 mol after the peak.
claims.quote: The heating coil method shows the highest peak gas generation at 7.72 mol, significantly exceeding the heating plate and rod methods. Comparing peak gas generation from different heating methods with the corresponding peak pressure, peak temperature, and ambient temperature in Fig. 6 reveals a clear relationship. Peak pressure and ambient temperature rise with increased gas generation, while peak temperature after thermal runaway decreases.

claims.claim: The heating rod method produced the widest flammable concentration range (51.11%) and the lowest lower flammability limit (7.97%) among the three heating methods, making its released mixed gases the most dangerous.
claims.quote: the flammable concentration ranges from largest to smallest for the three heating methods is: heating rod (51.11 %), heating coil (49.82 %), and heating plate (46.94 %). Furthermore, the lower flammability limit is the lowest for the heating rod (7.97 %), followed by the heating plate (8.26 %) and the heating coil (8.88 %).

claims.claim: Under the same 100% SOC conditions, CO2 concentration in thermal runaway gases decreased in the order: heating coil (27.73%), heating plate (24.6%), and heating rod (18.08%), with H2 concentration showing the inverse order: heating rod (25.06%), heating plate (24.33%), heating coil (23.61%).
claims.quote: the descending order of CO2 gas concentrations is as follows: heating coil (27.73 %), heating plate (24.6 %), and heating rod (18.08 %). In contrast to the changes in CO2 gas concentration, the descending order of H2 gas concentrations from high to low is heating rod (25.06 %), heating plate (24.33 %), and heating coil (23.61 %).

claims.claim: Cell safety does not directly connect to non-flammability of electrolytes based on the thermal runaway mechanism.
claims.quote: Cell safety does not directly connect to non-flammability of electrolytes based on the thermal runaway mechanism.

claims.claim: Due to the lack of carbonate structure, ionic liquids produce much less CO2 compared to conventional carbonate electrolytes, which might be an advantage for cell safety.
claims.quote: Due to the lack of carbonate structure, ionic liquids produce much less CO2, which might be an advantage of ionic liquids for the cell safety.

claims.claim: Small exothermic peaks starting at about 120°C in lithium-ion cells can be ascribed to the chemical reduction of ionic liquids by LixC6 anode when partial destruction of the SEI film occurs.
claims.quote: Small exothermic peaks started at about 120oC can be ascribed to the chemical reduction of ionic liquids by LixC6 anode, when the partial destruction of the SEI film occurs.

claims.claim: The chemical oxidation of ionic liquid by LiyNi1/3Mn1/3Co1/3O2 cathode began at about 220°C, with more violent heat generation occurring around 280°C due to combustion reactions of electrolyte and separator based on released oxygen from the cathode.
claims.quote: The chemical oxidation of ionic liquid by LiyNi1/3Mn1/3Co1/3O2 cathode began at about 220°C and more violent heat generation occurred around 280°C. The first exothermic reaction is similar to that on the anode, however, the second one was more vigorous due to the combustion reactions of electrolyte and separator based on the released oxygen from the cathode.

claims.claim: Inorganic anions in ionic liquid electrolytes made the onset temperatures of exothermic reactions lower and total enthalpies larger compared to organic anion (TFSI) electrolytes.
claims.quote: Inorganic anions made the onset temperatures lower and total enthalpies larger.

claims.claim: The effective negative-electrode solid-phase diffusion coefficient (Dsn) and reaction rate constant (kn) decrease monotonically with cycle number, whereas the other three parameters (D1, Dsp, kp) did not follow any particular trend.
claims.quote: The effective negative-electrode solid-phase diffusion coefﬁcient and reaction rate constant (Dsn and kn) decrease monotonically with cycle #, whereas the other three parameters did not follow any particular trend

claims.claim: Voltage-discharge curves were very sensitive to the value of the effective solid-phase diffusion coefficient Dsn but weakly sensitive to deviations in D1, Dsp, kp, and kn from their nominal values, resulting in large uncertainties in those four parameters when fit to experimental data.
claims.quote: The voltage-discharge curves were very sensitive to the value of the effective solid-phase diffusion coefﬁcient Dsn but weakly sensitive to deviations in the model parameters D1, Dsp, kp, and kn from their nominal values, resulting in large uncertainties in their values when ﬁt to experimental voltage-discharge curves

claims.claim: The probability density functions for Dsn at different cycle numbers have minimal overlap, providing very high confidence that the monotonic reduction of the effective solid-phase diffusion coefficient with increasing cycle number is statistically significant.
claims.quote: The pdfs for Dsn at different cycle numbers have minimal overlap, providing very high conﬁdence that the monotonic reduction of the effective solid-phase diffusion coefﬁcient with increase cycle number is statistically signiﬁcant.

claims.claim: Tracking cycle-dependent variations in effective transport and electrochemical kinetics parameters is valid only for a particular protocol of galvanostatic charge and discharge, and is not appropriate for use in the design of lithium-ion batteries with reduced capacity fade.
claims.quote: Tracking cycle-dependent variations in the effective values for transport and electrochemical kinetics is valid only for a particular protocol of galvanostatic charge and discharge, and is not appropriate for use in the design of lithium-ion batteries with reduced capacity fade.

claims.claim: Discretizing the cathode, separator, and anode into 50 equally spaced node points results in 600 Differential Algebraic Equations to be solved in the finite difference model, which is impractical for real-time simulation.
claims.quote: The discretization of the cathode, separator, and anode into 50 equally spaced node points would result in 600 Differential Algebraic Equations (DAEs) to be solved in the ﬁnite difference model, which is impractical for real-time simulation.

claims.claim: The TR propagation time of the LFP module decreased from 1218 s to 603 s (a reduction of 50.1%) when pressure decreased from 95 kPa to 35 kPa, while the NCM523 module TR propagation time increased from 33 s to 151 s (an increase of 357.6%) over the same pressure range.
claims.quote: The TR time of the LiFePO4 module decreases from 1218 to 603 s, when the pressure decreases from 95 to 35 kPa, but the LiNi0:5Co0:2Mn0:3O2 module increases from 33 to 151 s, indicating a reduction in the TR propagation time of 50.1% for the LiFePO4 module but an increase of 357.6% for the LiNi0:5Co0:2Mn0:3O2 module.

claims.claim: As ambient pressure decreases from 95 kPa to 35 kPa, the average maximum temperature of LIB modules decreases by 20–50 °C and TR behaviors become weaker.
claims.quote: The results indicate that TR behaviors become weaker and the average maximum temperature of modules decreases 20e50 (cid:1)C as the pressure decreases.

claims.claim: The average maximum temperature of the NCM523 module is approximately twice that of the LFP module under the same pressure environment, indicating a more serious thermal hazard for NCM523.
claims.quote: The T d j of NCM523 module is twice the temperature of the LFP module at the same pressure. The thermal hazard of the NCM523 module is more serious, which is closely related to the cathode material of the battery.

claims.claim: Lower environmental pressure accelerates TR propagation speed in the LFP module but inhibits it in the NCM523 module; specifically, LFP propagation speed at 35 kPa (0.42 cell/min) is twice that at 95 kPa, while NCM523 propagation speed at 95 kPa (6.00 cell/min) is 3.57 times that at 35 kPa.
claims.quote: lower environmental pressure can signiﬁcantly accelerate the propagation speed of the LFP module, but this inhibits the propagation of the NCM523 module. Speciﬁcally, the TR propagation speed was 0.42 cell/min with the LFP module at 35 kPa, which is twice as fast as the module at 95 kPa. The TR propagation speed was 6.00 cell/min with the NCM523 module at 95 kPa, which was 3.57 times as fast as the module at 35 kPa.

claims.claim: As ambient pressure decreases, the impact force of the NCM523 battery safety venting increases due to a larger instantaneous pressure difference between the battery interior and the environment, making impact protection critical for NCM523 modules at low pressures.
claims.quote: As the ambient pressure decreased, the instantaneous pressure difference between the battery and the environment increased, so the impact force of the NCM523 battery grew. When the NCM523 battery was used in a lower pressure environment, the impact force could increase signiﬁcantly. Thus, the impact protection is very important for the NCM523 module at low pressures.

claims.claim: A temperature above 100°C within a lithium-ion battery cell initiates exothermic side reactions causing thermal runaway with devastating effects such as fire and explosion.
claims.quote: a temperature above 100°C within the cell initiates exothermic side reactions, causing thermal runaway with devastating effects such as fire and explosion.

claims.claim: At temperatures 25–50°C above room temperature, internal resistance and capacity fading processes associated with aging accelerate considerably and reduce battery life.
claims.quote: At temperatures 25–50◦C above room temperature, internal resistance and capacity fading processes associated with aging have been proven to accelerate considerably and reduce battery life.

claims.claim: The time for local maximum temperature to reach 120°C was 8.2 s in scenario 1 (external short circuit), 9.2 s in scenario 2 (nail penetration), and 10.7 s in scenario 3 (impurity-induced short circuit).
claims.quote: The time it takes for the temperature to reach 120◦C is around 10 s in all scenarios; in scenario 1 it is 8.2 s, in scenario 2 9.2 s and in scenario 3 10.7 s.
