claims.claim: The lithium-ion concentration in the electrolyte approaches zero at the interface between the positive electrode and the separator in all three short-circuit scenarios, with this mass transport limitation reached around 50 ms after short circuit in scenarios 1 and 2, and about 150 ms in scenario 3.
claims.quote: The concentration approaches zero at the interface between the positive electrode and the separator in all scenarios, thus showing that a mass transport-limited current is reached. It happens very rapidly: In scenarios 1 and 2 around 50 ms and in scenario 3 about 150 ms after short circuit.

claims.claim: Cooling applied only from the sides (case 1) delayed the time to reach 120°C by only about 0.1 s compared to adiabatic conditions, because the cell-layer length was too long for efficient heat removal, creating a hotspot in the middle.
claims.quote: In case 1, the time it takes for the local maximum temperature to reach 120◦C deviates very little, about 0.1 s for all scenarios, compared to under adiabatic conditions. This is because the length (x) of the layer is too long to allow for an efﬁcient removal of heat, which creates a hotspot at the location furthest away from the sides.

claims.claim: Reducing the state of charge from 100% to 50% at short-circuit initiation resulted in slower temperature rise to 120°C: 10 s in scenario 1, 11 s in scenario 2, and 15.1 s in scenario 3, yet the current over time did not differ markedly compared to the fully charged case, indicating mass transport in the electrolyte remained the rate-determining process.
claims.quote: The results show a slower rise in local maximum temperature to 120◦C for all scenarios: In scenario 1 it is 10 s, in scenario 2 11 s and in scenario 3 15.1 s... the current over time does not differ markedly compared to the fully charged case. Thus, the mass transport in the electrolyte is still the rate determining process for the temperature increase.

claims.claim: Lower cathode porosity results in higher tortuosity, which impedes Li+ ion transport in the electrolyte, thereby increasing charge transport resistance and leading to increased ohmic heat.
claims.quote: Lower porosity results in higher tortuosity of the cathode, which impedes Li+ ion transport in the electrolyte, thereby increasing the charge transport resistance. This high internal resistance increases the polarization of Li+ concentration and electrolyte potential. Consequently, according to Eq. 13, steep concentration and potential gradients ensue, leading to increased ohmic heat.

claims.claim: The maximum ohmic heating rate occurs in the vicinity of the cathode-separator interface due to higher concentration and potential gradients near that interface.
claims.quote: It is evident that the maximum ohmic heating rate occurs in the vicinity of the cathode-separator interface. This is due to the existence of higher concentration and potential gradients close to the separator-electrode interface.

claims.claim: Tortuosity significantly affects joule heating rate, while its influence on reaction and reversible heat rates is quite negligible.
claims.quote: It is important to note that tortuosity significantly affects joule heating rate, while its influence on reaction and reversible heat rates is quite negligible.

claims.claim: At high discharge rates, a low-porosity cathode (ε ≈ 0.18–0.20) causes cell self-shutdown before full discharge due to large internal resistance, which limits the maximum temperature reached even at higher C-rates.
claims.quote: The lower maximum temperature for the cell with cathode porosity of ε ∼0.18–0.20 is due to the self-shutdown of the cell. At high discharge rate, because of large internal resistance of a low-porosity cathode, the cell shuts down before the full discharge in the operating window of 3.0 - 4.2 V.

claims.claim: When the discharge current is below 7A, the maximum cell temperature within a cathode porosity range of ε ≈ 0.18–0.4 can remain outside the unsafe zone (T > 80°C) at ambient temperature of 25°C; above 7A, the cell can reach the unsafe temperature region for the same porosity range.
claims.quote: Figure 6a suggests that when the discharge current is below 7A, the maximum cell temperature rise within a cathode porosity range of ε ∼ 0.4–0.18 can remain outside the unsafe zone. However, the cell can reach the unsafe temperature region, for the same cathode porosity range, when the discharge current is higher than 7A.

claims.claim: The probability of thermal runaway in commercial lithium-ion cells type 18650 grows with increasing number of charge/discharge cycles and increasing state of charge (SOC).
claims.quote: a probability of thermal runaway in commercial lithium-ion cells of the type 18650 grows with number increase of charge/discharge cycles and increase of cells state of charge (SOC)

claims.claim: With increasing number of charge/discharge cycles, the initiation temperature of exothermic reactions of thermal runaway decreases considerably and the released energy increases.
claims.quote: with the number growth of cells charge/discharge cycles, it is observed a considerable decline of an initiation temperature of exothermic reactions of thermal runaway and increase of released energy

claims.claim: For cells with NC=45 cycles, the first group of exothermic reactions initiates at approximately 32°C and reaches a first maximum at approximately 116°C, releasing 3.29 kJ of energy.
claims.quote: the exothermic reactions of the ﬁrst group (Fig. 2) appear in the temperature range from 32 to 116°C... Energy of exothermic reactions of ﬁrst group... 45 [cycles]... 3.29

claims.claim: The energy released by the first group of exothermic reactions does not depend on SOC but increases with the number of charge/discharge cycles: 0 kJ at NC=0, 1.08 kJ at NC=15, 2.14 kJ at NC=30, and 3.29 kJ at NC=45.
claims.quote: Temperature ranges of the ﬁrst group of the exothermic reactions do not depend on SOC (Table I and Fig. 2). Hence, also values of the energy released by the exothermic reactions of the ﬁrst group do not depend on SOC. However, they depend heavily on a number of cell charge/discharge cycles (Table I).

claims.claim: The gas released during the first group of exothermic reactions consists of more than 99% hydrogen, with other gases (CO2, CO, CH4, C2H4) accounting for less than 1%, and the amount of hydrogen released does not depend on SOC but increases with the number of charge/discharge cycles.
claims.quote: The conducted measuring showed that the obtained gas from a cells consisted of hydrogen (more than 99%), while the other gases (CO2, CO, CH4 and C2H4) accounted for less than 1%... a hydrogen amount released from a cell does not depend on SOC but heavily depend on a number of charge/discharge cycles.

claims.claim: For cells with NC=45 cycles, the current interrupt device activates at approximately 75–85°C regardless of SOC, whereas for cells with NC=0, it activates at approximately 115–150°C depending on SOC, indicating greater gas release in aged cells.
claims.quote: For cells with NC = 0 (which have no exothermic reactions of the ﬁrst group, Fig. 2), the current interrupt device is activated at temperature values approximately 115–150◦C depending on SOC, Fig. 3c. For cells with NC = 45 (at which the exothermic reactions of the ﬁrst group do take place, Fig. 2), the current interrupt device is activated approximately at the temperature 75–85◦C regardless of SOC

claims.claim: The recombination of atomic hydrogen (H + H → H2) is an exothermic reaction with heat emission of 436 kJ/mole, which is more intensive than the heat of hydrogen burning in oxygen at 285.8 kJ/mole.
claims.quote: The reaction (Eq. 3) is a powerful exothermic reaction with heat emission 436 kJ/mole (hydrogen). This heat emission is more intensive than in the reaction of hydrogen burning in oxygen and is equal to 285.8 kJ/mole (hydrogen).

claims.claim: With increasing hydrogen amount accumulated in graphite, the activation energy decreases, causing the initiation temperature of the thermal runaway exothermic reaction to decrease.
claims.quote: As with a hydrogen amount growth in graphite, its activation energy must decrease, so also an initial temperature of the exothermic reaction of the thermal runaway must decreases

claims.claim: After 45 charge/discharge cycles at 0°C, the discharge capacity of 18650 NCM/Graphite cells fell to 70% of their initial discharge capacity, which is considered the end of cell lifetime.
claims.quote: after 45 cycles of charge/discharge (at T = 0◦C), their discharge capacity fell down to 70% of their initial discharge capacity... Usually, the decrease of the cells discharge capacity down to 70% of their initial discharge capacity is considered to be the end of cells lifetime.

claims.claim: With increasing SOC, the temperature of thermal runaway shifts to lower values because cathode thermal stability decreases and oxygen release from the cathode increases.
claims.quote: with growth of SOC, the temperature of the thermal runaway shifts to the side of lower values. This is connected with the fact that with the SOC growth, a cathode thermal stability falls, while oxygen release from a cathode grows.

claims.claim: The SEI layer begins to decompose at 90 to 120°C, with the SEI reaction peaking at a temperature of 100°C as measured by ARC and DSC.
claims.quote: the solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer, which protects the anode active material from direct reaction with the electrolyte solvent, will begin to decompose at 90 to 120◦C. Using accelerated rate calorimetry (ARC) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), it has been shown that the SEI reaction peaks at a temperature of 100◦C.

claims.claim: The heat release from SEI decomposition was independent of the amount of intercalated lithium but was very sensitive to the surface area of the anode.
claims.quote: It was shown that the heat release was independent of the amount of intercalated lithium but was very sensitive to the surface area of the anode, as this increased the amount of meta-stable SEI.

claims.claim: Once the SEI layer decomposes, intercalated lithium in the anode begins to react with electrolyte solvents at temperatures greater than 120°C.
claims.quote: Once the SEI layer decomposes, it no longer protects the graphite and the intercalated lithium in the anode will begin to react with the electrolyte solvents at temperatures greater than 120◦C.

claims.claim: The positive active material can decompose and release oxygen and/or react with electrolyte solvent beginning at approximately 170°C, after the anode reactions heat the cell.
claims.quote: The first two reactions in the anode can heat the cell to temperatures where the positive active material can decompose and release oxygen and/or react with the electrolyte solvent, beginning at approximately 170◦C.

claims.claim: Electrolyte decomposition begins at temperatures greater than 200°C, and the combined heat from decomposition and combustion with released oxygen can elevate cell temperatures to over 600°C.
claims.quote: any electrolyte that remains after the reactions with the negative and positive electrodes can decompose at temperatures greater than 200◦C. The heat generated from electrolyte decomposition combined with heat released during combustion with released oxygen can elevate cell temperatures to over 600◦C.

claims.claim: Increasing the solution-phase volume fraction of the negative electrode (εs,n) from 0.3 to 0.7 shifts the discharge curve from right to left and decreases battery capacity.
claims.quote: the increase in the solution-phase volume fraction shifts the discharge curve from right to left and decreases the battery capacity.

claims.claim: The electronic conductivity of the positive and negative electrodes has a significant effect on the IR drop only at high current densities.
claims.quote: The electronic conductivity of the positive and negative electrodes each has a significant effect on the IR drop only at high current densities.

claims.claim: Using the heuristic algorithm with a divide-and-conquer strategy, the complete identification of P2D model parameters can be performed within 10 hours using a single core computer.
claims.quote: With the heuristic algorithm, the full set of P2D parameters can be performed within 10 h using a single core computer.

claims.claim: A prior method using a genetic algorithm required three weeks to complete identification of 88 parameters using a cluster of five quad-core computers.
claims.quote: It took three weeks to complete an 88 parameters identiﬁcation using a cluster of ﬁve quad-core computers and a genetic algorithm.

claims.claim: The percentile error of simulated results with identified parameters was 78.1 mV or less, and the average error was approximately 12.9 mV or less across discharge rates of 0.1C, 0.5C, 1C, 2C, and 3C.
claims.quote: The percentile error of the simulated results with the identiﬁed parameters is 78.1 mV or less, and the average error of the simulated results is about 12.9 mV or less.

claims.claim: Isolated solid electrolytic salts (Cases 7 and 8 in Table III) exhibit high activation energy values that prevent the initiation of a thermal runaway, whereas experiments involving organic solvents show significantly smaller EA values, implying that organic solvents act as promoters of the runaway event.
claims.quote: comparing the values obtained from the different DSC proﬁles as published by different authors for solid samples of electrolytes, it can be derived that the high EA values evidenced in the presence of isolated solid electrolytic salts (Cases 7 and 8 in Table III) prevent the initiation of a thermal runaway. Experiments involving organic solvents on the other hand, where EA values for the reaction global rate were signiﬁcantly smaller, could imply that organic solvents act as promoters of the runaway event.

claims.claim: LiPF6 could act as the limiting reactant which, via its decomposition, controls the series of runaway reactions, independent of the cathodic material present.
claims.quote: it can be concluded that independent of the cathodic material, LiPF6 could act as the limiting reactant which via its decomposition controls the series of runaway reactions.

claims.claim: The total loss of cooling capacity and overheating above temperatures of design and control will drive a thermally autocatalytic runaway of a series of reactions, representing the worst-case scenario for the battery.
claims.quote: the total loss of cooling capacity and overheating above temperatures of design and control, which will drive to a thermally autocatalytic runaway of a series of reactions, is the worst-case scenario for the battery

claims.claim: In Criticality Class V, values of pressure and temperature reach maximum values inside the cell, cooling capacity as a barrier is null, and venting with flammable and toxic gas release increases with time until gas ignition and pressure release by explosion.
claims.quote: Loss of control with respect to the expected normal operation temperature. Values of pressure and temperature reach maximum values inside the cell. Cooling capacity as barrier is null at this point. Venting event, ﬂammable and toxic gas release increases with time. Critical point is reached when gas catches ﬁre and pressure is release by explosion event.

claims.claim: The calculated orders of reaction n for LiPF6 across different data sets were found to lie between 0.57 and 1.38, making it reasonable to treat the decomposition as first order.
claims.quote: The calculated orders of reaction, n, for LiPF6 in different data set results were found to lie between 0.57 and 1.38; thus it is reasonable to treat the decomposition as ﬁrst order.

claims.claim: For NMC cathodes, reducing carbon black content from 5.88 to 4.00 wt% and CB:B ratio to CB:B=1 leads to significantly increased specific electronic resistances across all mass loading configurations.
claims.quote: The speciﬁc electronic resistances of NMC cathodes measured in this study conﬁrm this ﬁnding as they are signiﬁcantly lower at high carbon black (CB) contents and CB:B > 1 for all mass loading (ML) conﬁgurations

claims.claim: For NMC cathodes with high mass loading, specific discharge capacity approaches 0 mAh/g for non-calendered cathodes at 5C, whereas low mass loading cathodes show only about 50% deterioration compared to the high calendering configuration.
claims.quote: At low mass loadings the deterioration of speciﬁc discharge capacity for medium and low calendering conﬁgurations is about 50% compared to the conﬁguration with high calendering stress load. However, this deterioration is still relatively small compared to the cells with high mass loadings where speciﬁc discharge capacity even approaches 0 mAh/g for non calendered cathodes.

claims.claim: Ionic conductivity limitations are expected to become significant only at calendering stress loads beyond 22% for NMC and 30% for LMO, within the manufacturing parameters studied.
claims.quote: Limits of ion transport which are expected to occur in electrodes which are calendered too strongly, appear to become only signiﬁcant at calendering stress load beyond 22% for NMC and 30% for LMO.

claims.claim: Increasing calendering stress load reduces standard deviations of discharge capacities among identically produced cells, with the largest improvement occurring between the non-calendered and medium calendering level.
claims.quote: By far the highest standard deviations of discharge capacities were observed among cells with non calendered cathodes. The deviations decrease with the increase of calendering stress load. The largest improvement occurs between non calendering and the medium calendering level.

claims.claim: NMC cathodes show higher specific discharge capacities at low C-rates, while LMO cathodes show higher specific discharge capacities at high C-rates, with the crossover C-rate shifting toward higher values as calendering stress load increases.
claims.quote: NMC has higher speciﬁc discharge capacities at low C-rates and LMO shows higher speciﬁc discharge capacities at higher C-rates... the C-rate above which LMO becomes superior to NMC can be shifted toward higher C-rates by increasing the calendering stress load.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway is defined as a phenomenon that occurs when the heat generation rate exceeds 1 °C/min.
claims.quote: thermal runaway, which can be defined as a phenomenon that occurs when the heat generation rate exceeds 1 ◦C min–1.

claims.claim: The DSC measurement of the separator showed two endothermic reactions at approximately 125°C and 155°C, corresponding to pore-closing and thermal-shrinkage deformation, respectively.
claims.quote: two endothermic reactions occurring at around 125 ◦C (the first reaction indicated by (i) in the figure) and 155 ◦C (the second reaction indicated by (ii) in the figure) could be observed. Based on the DSC results... the first and second endothermic reactions depicted in Fig. 1(a) were considered to be a pore-closing reaction and thermal-shrinkage deformation, respectively.

claims.claim: At approximately 125°C, a rapid increase in internal resistance by almost two orders of magnitude and a reduction in OCV by a few mV were observed, caused by separator shutdown.
claims.quote: A rapid increase in the internal resistance by almost two orders of magnitude and a reduction in the OCV by a few mV were observed at point A (approximately 125 ◦C). Based the first endothermic reaction of DSC in Fig. 1(a), this rapid change in the internal resistance was potentialy caused by a separator shutdown, which completely closed the pore.

claims.claim: Shrinkage of the separator induced an internal short circuit, which triggered Joule heat, resulting in self-heating observed at 175°C in the ARC test.
claims.quote: the shrinkage of the separator induced an internal short circuit, which was further considered to trigger Joule heat, thereby resulting in the self-heating observed at 175 ◦C.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway in the LFP battery required a temperature above 220°C and could not occur from Joule heat alone; additional heating was necessary.
claims.quote: thermal runaway did not occur owing to this process alone, as thermal runaway requires a temperature above 220 ◦C that necessitates additional heating.

claims.claim: For NCA 18650 cells, overcharged conditions at 132% and 143% SoC showed onset temperatures of thermal runaway as low as 80°C and 65°C, respectively, attributed to lithium deposition on the anode.
claims.quote: For NCA cell, the overcharged conditions showed onset temperatures as low as 80°C and 65°C for 132% and 143% SoC levels, respectively. The reason for this substantial drop in onset temperature in case of the overcharged cells was attributed to the lithium deposition on the anode which compromised the thermal stability.

claims.claim: Aged Li-ion cells (70% SoH, NCM/graphite 18650) showed onset of exothermic reactions shifted approximately 30°C lower than fresh cells, and unlike fresh cells, aged cells did not show any variation with SoC level.
claims.quote: the OER for the aged cells showed a strong shift of temperature to nearly 30°C (red curves in Fig. 15), which marked the lowest onset temperature of all the experiments. The reason for this shift was attributed to the high surface area lithium (HSAL) metal deposition on the anode surface of the aged cells... Moreover, unlike fresh cells, aged cells did not show any variation with the SoC level.

claims.claim: The fully discharged LCO Li-ion cell (0% SoC) did not show any sign of thermal runaway, while cells with SoC levels above 50% showed substantially lower onset temperatures and exponentially increasing self-heating rates.
claims.quote: the fully discharged cell (0% SoC) did not show any sign of thermal runaway which clearly explained the severity of thermal effects of a battery under operation... the SoC level of more than 50% has been observed to have substantial impact on thermal instability and hence on thermal runaway behavior of the battery.

claims.claim: Under one-sided heating of a prismatic 50 Ah LMO cell, the critical temperature for thermal runaway was substantially delayed to 281°C due to the large temperature gradient in the cell, compared to 192°C for both-sided heating with internal short circuit.
claims.quote: Due to the large temperature gradient in the cell, the thermal runaway was substantially delayed, and the critical temperature was observed at 281°C.

claims.claim: Mini-channel cooling fails to prevent thermal runaway in a nail-penetrated (short-circuited) cell even at high coolant flow rates, but can effectively prevent heat propagation from the short-circuited cell to neighboring cells at adequate flow rates.
claims.quote: the mini-channel cooling fails to prevent the thermal runaway in the short-circuited cell even by employing high flow rates of the coolant through the channels. However, as depicted in Fig. 36c, propagation of heat from one cell to the neighboring cells could be efficiently prevented by employing adequate flow rate of the coolant

claims.claim: Before the battery temperature approaches the uncontrollable temperature, the gas release of LFP, LMO, and NCM batteries during stage B is 0.094 mol, 0.042 mol, and 0.058 mol, respectively.
claims.quote: the gas released from the LFP battery is 0.094 mol, the gas released from the LMO battery is 0.042 mol, and the gas released from the NCM battery is 0.058 mol.

claims.claim: The gas released before safety venting accounts for 29.1%, 75.2%, and 55.4% of the total gas causing the safety valve to open in LFP, LMO, and NCM batteries, respectively.
claims.quote: the gas release which causes the safety venting, accounts for 29.1, 75.2 and 55.4% of the total gas in LFP, LMO and NCM batteries, respectively.

claims.claim: Safety venting of the LFP battery is mainly due to electrolyte volatilization, whereas safety venting of LMO and NCM batteries is mainly caused by gas release.
claims.quote: the safety venting of the LFP battery is mainly due to the volatilization of the electrolyte. However, the safety venting of the LMO and NCM batteries is mainly caused by the gas release.

claims.claim: The NCM battery has the greatest total pressure change during thermal runaway at 9.817 MPa, which is 4.3 times the pressure change of the LFP battery.
claims.quote: The NCM battery has the greatest pressure change, P = 9.817 MPa, which is 4.3 times the pressure change of the LFP battery.

claims.claim: For the LFP battery after thermal runaway, gas release is the main cause of structural change in the cathode material, whereas for LMO and NCM batteries, impact force is the dominant cause of cathode material structural change.
claims.quote: For the LFP battery, gas release is the main source, and for LMO and NCM batteries, the impact force is the dominant cause.
